title: Pucker Up for Cranberry Day!
story: Get ready to make some seriously sour faces! Every November 23, people celebrate National Eat a Cranberry Day by biting into these tiny red superfruits. Some brave souls eat them completely raw and watch their friends' hilarious reactions. Others mix cranberries into muffins, smoothies, or trail mix to tame the tartness. Many families use this day to prep cranberry sauce for upcoming holiday meals, turning the sour challenge into something deliciously sweet.
activity_question: How do some people make cranberries less sour on this holiday?
activity_choices: Mix them into muffins or smoothies
activity_answer: Mix them into muffins or smoothies
bonus_fact: One cranberry contains only 4 calories but packs tons of vitamin C!

title: Going Nuts for National Cashew Day!
story: Cashews get their own special day every November 23! People celebrate by munching on these creamy, curved nuts straight from the bag, tossing them into stir-fries, or blending them into smooth cashew butter. Bakeries sell cashew cookies and candy makers create cashew brittle. Social media fills with photos of cashew recipes and fun facts about how these kidney-shaped treats actually grow hanging from cashew apples on trees!
activity_question: According to the story, what unusual way do cashews grow?
activity_choices: They hang from cashew apples on trees
They grow inside hard coconut shells
They sprout directly from tree bark
activity_answer: They hang from cashew apples on trees
bonus_fact: Cashews are never sold in their shells because the shells contain toxic oils!

title: Fibonacci Day: Numbers That Add Up to Fun!
story: Every November 23, math lovers celebrate Fibonacci Day because the date (11/23) matches the famous Fibonacci sequence: 1, 1, 2, 3! This special number pattern starts with 1, 1, then each new number is made by adding the two before it. People share Fibonacci spirals on social media, hunt for the sequence in nature like sunflower seeds and pinecones, and teachers organize fun math games. Some families even arrange snacks in Fibonacci numbers - like 1 apple, 1 orange, 2 crackers, 3 grapes! It's the perfect day to discover how this amazing pattern appears everywhere around us.
activity_question: How do you make the next number in the Fibonacci sequence?
activity_choices: Add the two numbers before it
activity_answer: Add the two numbers before it
follow_up_question: What other patterns from nature have you noticed in your backyard or neighborhood?

title: Time Lords Take Over November 23!
story: Every November 23, fans around the world celebrate Doctor Who Day by honoring the longest-running science fiction TV show ever made! This special day marks when the very first Doctor Who episode aired way back in 1963. Today, fans called "Whovians" throw viewing parties to watch their favorite episodes featuring the time-traveling Doctor and the famous blue police box called the TARDIS. They dress up as different versions of the Doctor, complete with bow ties, long scarves, or funky glasses. Some fans even build their own TARDIS replicas! Social media fills up with quotes like "Allons-y!" and "Geronimo!" as people share their love for this timey-wimey adventure show that has been thrilling audiences for decades.
activity_question: What do Doctor Who fans call themselves?
bonus_fact: The TARDIS stands for "Time And Relative Dimension In Space."

title: Japan's Thank You Day for Workers
story: Every November 23, Japan celebrates Labor Thanksgiving Day to honor all the hardworking people who keep the country running! Kids make special thank-you cards and drawings for police officers, firefighters, doctors, and even garbage collectors. Families visit local workers to say "arigatou gozaimasu" (thank you very much) and give them handmade gifts. It's like having a giant appreciation party for everyone who helps make life better!
activity_question: What do Japanese kids make on Labor Thanksgiving Day?
follow_up_question: Which workers in your community would you want to thank and why?
